The exiles have returned to a ruined city, yet their first act is not to rebuild their homes or fortify their walls—it is to build an altar. Ezra shows us a people who understand that before anything else can be restored, their relationship with God must be restored. Jeshua the priest and Zerubbabel the civil leader move together immediately, even while surrounded by hostile populations and before the temple's foundation is even laid. They do not wait for perfect circumstances. They do not negotiate with fear. They set up the altar "on its foundation" and begin the daily sacrifices that the Law prescribed. This is the grammar of trust: they are saying, through their actions, that God's presence and God's requirements matter more than their vulnerability does.

What follows reveals the depth of their commitment. They celebrate the Feast of Tabernacles according to the written pattern. They establish the regular rhythm of offerings—morning and evening, monthly, seasonal, and voluntary gifts. They have been away for seventy years, yet the structure of Israel's worship remains clear in their memory and in the written Word. They are not improvising; they are submitting. And while they do this, merchants from Sidon and Tyre are contracted to bring cedar from Lebanon, materials are gathered, leadership is appointed, and the groundwork is laid for the actual building to begin. Worship and work go together—not one before the other, but both essential.

The moment the foundation is laid, something extraordinary happens. The priests in their official garments and the Levites with their instruments arrange themselves exactly as King David had prescribed, and they sing the ancient refrain: "For He is good; for His loving devotion to Israel endures forever." Then the people erupt in praise. But the noise contains something that no victory cry can contain: the old priests and elders weep. They remember the first temple. They remember its glory. They see this foundation and know that what will rise here will not match what was lost. The text does not hide this mixture—the shouts of joy and the cries of grief become so loud that "the people could not distinguish" between them. Both are real. Both are true. Both are necessary.

This is what it means to return from exile: to rebuild not with the confidence of the beginning, but with the humility of the restored. The exiles have learned something in Babylon that the pre-exile generation may not have fully grasped—that God's presence is not secured by a building, that worship matters more than grandeur, that obedience in small things while surrounded by hostility is a greater trust than building in security. The noise of that day—mingled joy and lament—still echoes across centuries, reminding us that faith sometimes means celebrating what God is doing while grieving what we have lost, and trusting Him anyway.
